1. Home
  2. /Secondary Curriculum
  3. /IB
  4. /Biology

IB Biology

IB Biology HL and SL — content consolidation, data response technique, extended writing, all option topics, and IA support for Medicine and Veterinary Science.

Book Free AssessmentView All Biology Courses
OVERVIEW

What is IB Biology?

IB Biology is one of the most content-rich IB subjects, covering cell biology, molecular biology, genetics, ecology, evolution, and human physiology in significant depth. Higher Level Biology extends beyond SL with advanced content in nucleic acids, metabolism, plant biology, and animal physiology — content that can challenge even strong students without dedicated support. IB Biology is essential for students intending to study Medicine, Dentistry, Veterinary Science, Biomedical Sciences, or Environmental Science at university. In Dubai's IB schools, where Biology HL is frequently required for medical school applications, achieving a 6 or 7 is often the single most important outcome in the entire IB. At Improve ME, our IB Biology tutors combine thorough content delivery with the data response technique and extended writing skills that IB Biology examinations specifically reward.

IB
Qualification
Ages 16-18
Age range
SL & HL
Exam boards
COURSE CONTENT

IB Biology Course Content

SL & HL coverage plus Internal Assessment (IA) support and exam strategy.

Core Topics (HL and SL)

  • •Cell Biology — prokaryotic and eukaryotic cells, ultrastructure, membrane structure and transport, origin of cells, cell division (mitosis), stem cells and therapeutic cloning
  • •Molecular Biology — water and carbon in biology, carbohydrates and lipids, proteins (primary to quaternary structure), DNA structure and replication, transcription and translation, enzymes, cell respiration (aerobic and anaerobic), photosynthesis (light reactions and Calvin cycle)
  • •Genetics — chromosomes and genes, Mendelian inheritance (monohybrid and dihybrid), codominance, sex linkage, polygenic inheritance, Chi-squared test, karyotyping, mutations
  • •Ecology — species, communities, and ecosystems, energy flow and nutrient cycles (carbon, nitrogen, phosphorus), climate change, conservation, bioinformatics introduction
  • •Evolution and Biodiversity — evidence for evolution, natural selection, speciation (allopatric and sympatric), Hardy-Weinberg principle, classification, phylogenetics, cladistics
  • •Human Physiology — digestion and absorption, the blood system (heart structure and cardiac cycle), defence against infectious disease (innate and adaptive immunity, vaccination), gas exchange, neurons and synapses, hormones and homeostasis (osmoregulation, thermoregulation, blood glucose)

HL Additional Topics

  • •Nucleic Acids (HL) — DNA structure in detail, nucleosomes and chromatin, DNA replication (in depth), transcription and RNA processing, translation in detail, gene expression — regulation in prokaryotes and eukaryotes, methylation and acetylation, miRNA
  • •Metabolism (HL) — metabolic pathways, enzymes as catalysts, inhibition (competitive and non-competitive in depth), Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation in detail, light reactions and Calvin cycle in depth, chemiosmosis
  • •Plant Biology (HL) — plant structure, water transport (cohesion-tension), translocation (pressure flow), plant hormones (auxin, gibberellin, cytokinin, ethylene, ABA), plant reproduction
  • •Genetics and Evolution (HL) — meiosis in detail, recombination, genetic variation, gene pools, speciation mechanisms, molecular evolution, polyploidy
  • •Animal Physiology (HL) — hormonal and neural mechanisms in detail, osmoregulation across different environments, muscle structure and contraction, kidney function in detail, reproduction and gametogenesis

Internal Assessment, Options, and Data Skills

  • •Biology IA — individual investigation worth 20%; assessed on Personal Engagement, Exploration, Analysis, Evaluation, and Communication; must be a genuine student-designed investigation
  • •IA topic selection — selecting a biological topic with clear, measurable variables; avoiding overly simple or overly complex designs; ensuring sufficient biological context
  • •Data analysis skills — calculating means, standard deviations, standard error; t-tests and chi-squared tests; presenting data in appropriate graphs; interpreting biological significance of results
  • •Option topics — choose one: Neurobiology and Behaviour, Biotechnology and Bioinformatics, Ecology and Conservation, Human Physiology (each with detailed syllabus content)
  • •Extended response technique — structuring 6–9 mark questions with correct biological terminology, logical sequencing, and completeness
ASSESSMENT

Assessment Structure

We support both Internal Assessment (IA) and final exams with a structured plan, feedback cycles, and timed practice. SL and HL requirements are clearly differentiated.

Paper 1
Multiple Choice — Conceptual Recall
  • •SL: 45 mins, 20% — 30 MCQ; tests quick conceptual recall and eliminates common misconceptions; no data booklet on Paper 1.
  • •HL: 60 mins, 20% — 40 MCQ; same focus with greater breadth.
Paper 2
Short & Extended Response
  • •SL: 75 mins, 40%; HL: 135 mins, 36% — short and extended response; includes calculations, mechanism drawing (Chemistry), and 8–10 mark extended response questions requiring full working and explanation.
  • •Data booklet provided for Papers 2 and 3; students must know how to use it efficiently.
Paper 3 + IA
Data Analysis & Option + IA (20%)
  • •SL: Paper 3 — 60 mins, 20% (data analysis + option topic); HL: 75 mins, 24%.
  • •Section A: unfamiliar experimental data for analysis; processing raw data, graphs, gradients, evaluation of methodology.
  • •Biology IA: individual investigation worth 20%; assessed on Personal Engagement, Exploration, Analysis, Evaluation, and Communication.
EXAM BOARDS

Assessment Framework

IB DP (SL)
IB DP (HL)
Internal Assessment (IA)

Not sure which board your child is on? We'll check at your free assessment.

TEACHING APPROACH

Our IB Biology Teaching Approach

01

Content Consolidation with Active Recall

IB Biology contains more factual content than almost any other IB subject. We use active recall, concept mapping, and spaced repetition to ensure students retain information reliably — particularly for the HL additional topics that require deeper memorisation alongside understanding.

02

Data Response Technique

Paper 3 Section A presents unfamiliar experimental data and requires students to interpret it using biological knowledge. We train students to read axes carefully, identify trends, calculate from raw data, and apply relevant biology to explain results — a skill that rewards deliberate practice.

03

HL Extension Mastery

The HL additional topics — nucleic acids, metabolism, plant biology, and animal physiology — add substantial content that requires careful teaching rather than self-study. We dedicate significant session time to these areas, particularly the detailed molecular mechanisms in HL metabolism and genetics.

04

IA Design and Biology

A well-designed Biology IA requires a genuine experimental design with appropriate controls, sufficient trials, and statistically processable data. We help students design investigations that are both biologically interesting and practically achievable, and guide the full write-up process.

WHY CHOOSE US

Why Improve ME for IB Biology?

IB Biology specialists at both HL and SL
Full HL additional topics covered — Nucleic Acids, Metabolism, Plant Biology, Genetics and Evolution, Animal Physiology
All four option topics supported — Neurobiology, Biotechnology, Ecology, Human Physiology
IA design, data collection, analysis, and write-up support
Data response and experimental interpretation training for Paper 3
Extended response writing technique for Paper 2
Strong Medicine, Dentistry, and Veterinary Science applicant outcomes
Past paper programmes aligned to current IB Biology syllabus
one-to-one and small group sessions available
KHDA-approved tutoring centre at Gold & Diamond Park, Dubai
FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

Everything you need to know about Biology at Improve ME

Yes — HL Biology adds five substantial additional topic areas to the SL curriculum: Nucleic Acids, Metabolism, Plant Biology, Genetics and Evolution, and Animal Physiology. These HL topics go into significantly more molecular and physiological detail than the SL equivalents. HL students also face a longer Paper 2 with more extended response questions. The content load for HL Biology is very high and most students benefit significantly from dedicated tutoring support.
IB Biology requires students to calculate means, standard deviations, and standard errors, conduct t-tests and chi-squared tests, and interpret statistical significance. These skills appear in the IA (where they are essential for the Analysis criterion), in Paper 2 extended responses, and in Paper 3 data-based questions. We teach all required statistical skills as part of our Biology programme.
The Biology IA should be a student-designed experiment on a biological topic that genuinely interests them. The most important factors for a high-scoring IA are a clear, focused research question, a well-controlled experimental design, sufficient raw data, correct statistical analysis, and an evaluative conclusion that goes beyond listing sources of error. We support students through every stage and review the final write-up against all five IB criteria.
Option D (Human Physiology) is the most commonly chosen option in Dubai IB schools because it builds on familiar SL content and is perceived as the most accessible. However, all options are manageable with good preparation. The most important factor is that students know their option thoroughly — it represents significant Paper 3 marks.
Yes — we regularly support students who have fallen significantly behind, whether from the start of the IB or partway through Year 2. We conduct an honest initial assessment, prioritise the highest-mark topics for immediate attention, and build a structured recovery programme that addresses both content gaps and exam technique simultaneously.

1. Home

IB Chemistry

IB Chemistry HL and SL — organic mechanism precision, calculation mastery, all option topics, IA support, and strong outcomes for Medicine and Pharmacy applicants.

Book Free AssessmentView All Chemistry Courses
OVERVIEW

What is IB Chemistry?

IB Chemistry is one of the most demanding IB sciences, combining Physical, Organic, and Inorganic Chemistry with significant mathematical content and a rigorous Internal Assessment requirement. Higher Level Chemistry extends considerably beyond SL with advanced energetics, kinetics, organic mechanisms, and spectroscopic identification — content that approaches first-year university Chemistry in its depth. IB Chemistry is an essential prerequisite for Medicine, Dentistry, Pharmacy, Biochemistry, and Chemical Engineering at top universities. In Dubai's competitive IB school environment, achieving a 6 or 7 in Chemistry HL is often the determining factor for medical school offers. At Improve ME, our IB Chemistry tutors combine deep subject expertise with precise mark scheme knowledge to deliver consistently strong outcomes at both HL and SL.

IB
Qualification
Ages 16-18
Age range
SL & HL
Exam boards
COURSE CONTENT

IB Chemistry Course Content

SL & HL coverage plus Internal Assessment (IA) support and exam strategy.

Core Topics (HL and SL)

  • •Stoichiometry — mole concept, empirical and molecular formulae, limiting reagents, percentage yield and purity, concentration calculations, titration
  • •Atomic Structure — nuclear atom, electron configuration, emission spectra, ionisation energy trends
  • •Periodicity — periodic trends in atomic radius, ionic radius, ionisation energy, electronegativity, melting points; reactivity of Period 3 elements and oxides
  • •Chemical Bonding and Structure — ionic, covalent, metallic bonding; VSEPR theory and molecular geometry; intermolecular forces; polarity; giant structures vs molecular structures
  • •Energetics — standard enthalpy changes, Hess's Law, bond enthalpy, Born-Haber cycles (HL), entropy, Gibbs free energy (HL)
  • •Chemical Kinetics — rate of reaction, Maxwell-Boltzmann distribution, Arrhenius equation, rate expressions and order of reaction (HL), reaction mechanisms (HL)
  • •Equilibrium — dynamic equilibrium, Le Chatelier's principle, equilibrium constant expressions (Kc and Kp), acid-base equilibria, pH calculations, buffer solutions, Kw and Ka (HL)
  • •Acids and Bases — Brønsted-Lowry theory, strong and weak acids and bases, pH calculations, salt hydrolysis, indicators, titration curves

Organic Chemistry and HL Extensions

  • •Organic Chemistry Core — nomenclature and isomerism (structural, geometric, optical), functional group reactions, alkanes and alkenes, halogenoalkanes (nucleophilic substitution), alcohols, carbonyl compounds, carboxylic acids and esters
  • •Organic Chemistry HL — reaction mechanisms (SN1 and SN2 in depth, electrophilic addition, electrophilic aromatic substitution, nucleophilic acyl substitution), benzene chemistry, amines, diazonium salts, amino acids and proteins, condensation polymerisation
  • •Spectroscopy and Identification — mass spectrometry, IR spectroscopy (identifying functional groups), 1H NMR spectroscopy (chemical shift, integration, splitting patterns), combined spectral analysis (HL)
  • •Measurement and Data Processing — significant figures, uncertainty propagation, graphical analysis, percentage error, experimental design and evaluation

Internal Assessment and Option Topics

  • •Chemistry IA — individual investigation worth 20%; assessed on Personal Engagement, Exploration, Analysis, Evaluation, and Communication
  • •IA research question design — writing a focused, investigable RQ with clear independent and dependent variables and controlled variables
  • •Data collection and processing — recording raw data, calculating averages, propagating uncertainties, presenting processed data in tables and graphs
  • •Evaluation — discussing sources of error and uncertainty, evaluating the validity of the method, suggesting realistic improvements
  • •Option topics — choose one: Materials (metals, polymers, nanotechnology, condensation polymers), Biochemistry (proteins, carbohydrates, lipids, enzymes, vitamins), Energy (fossil fuels, nuclear, solar, electrochemical), Medicinal Chemistry (drug action, antiviral and antibacterial agents, stereochemistry of drugs)
  • •Option topic preparation — each option covers approximately 20 hours of teaching content and appears in Paper 3 Section B
ASSESSMENT

Assessment Structure

We support both Internal Assessment (IA) and final exams with a structured plan, feedback cycles, and timed practice. SL and HL requirements are clearly differentiated.

Paper 1
Multiple Choice — Conceptual Recall
  • •SL: 45 mins, 20% — 30 MCQ; tests quick conceptual recall and eliminates common misconceptions; no data booklet on Paper 1.
  • •HL: 60 mins, 20% — 40 MCQ; same focus with greater breadth.
Paper 2
Short & Extended Response
  • •SL: 75 mins, 40%; HL: 135 mins, 36% — short and extended response; includes calculations, mechanism drawing (Chemistry), and 8–10 mark extended response questions requiring full working and explanation.
  • •Data booklet provided for Papers 2 and 3; students must know how to use it efficiently.
Paper 3 + IA
Data Analysis & Option + IA (20%)
  • •SL: Paper 3 — 60 mins, 20% (data analysis + option topic); HL: 75 mins, 24%.
  • •Section A: unfamiliar experimental data for analysis; processing raw data, graphs, gradients, evaluation of methodology.
  • •Chemistry IA: individual investigation worth 20%; assessed on Personal Engagement, Exploration, Analysis, Evaluation, and Communication.
EXAM BOARDS

Assessment Framework

IB DP (SL)
IB DP (HL)
Internal Assessment (IA)

Not sure which board your child is on? We'll check at your free assessment.

TEACHING APPROACH

Our IB Chemistry Teaching Approach

01

HL/SL Content Distinction

HL Chemistry adds significant additional depth in energetics, kinetics, equilibrium, and organic chemistry. We teach HL and SL content separately and ensure HL students are fully prepared for the additional theoretical and mathematical demands of Paper 2.

02

Calculation Mastery

IB Chemistry is heavily calculation-based — pH, Kc, Kp, Gibbs free energy, Arrhenius, and stoichiometric calculations all appear across Papers 1 and 2. We drill these systematically with attention to method, significant figures, and units — the details that cost marks even when the chemistry is correct.

03

Mechanism Precision

Organic reaction mechanisms are the most heavily examined area of IB Chemistry HL and one of the most common sources of mark loss. We teach every mechanism until students can draw them perfectly with correct curly arrow notation, charges, and intermediate structures under exam conditions.

04

IA Excellence

A high-scoring Chemistry IA requires a well-designed investigation, precise data collection, correct uncertainty propagation, and an evaluative conclusion that goes beyond simply identifying errors. We support students through every stage and review drafts against all five IB criteria.

WHY CHOOSE US

Why Improve ME for IB Chemistry?

IB Chemistry specialists at both HL and SL
Organic mechanism mastery — SN1, SN2, electrophilic substitution, and all HL mechanisms
HL additional content — advanced energetics, kinetics, equilibrium, and spectroscopy
All four option topics supported — Materials, Biochemistry, Energy, Medicinal Chemistry
IA research question design, data collection, analysis, and write-up support
Calculation precision — pH, Kc, Kp, Gibbs, Arrhenius
Strong Medicine, Dentistry, and Pharmacy applicant outcomes in Dubai
Past paper programmes aligned to current IB Chemistry syllabus
one-to-one and small group sessions available
KHDA-approved tutoring centre at Gold & Diamond Park, Dubai
FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

Everything you need to know about Chemistry at Improve ME

IB Chemistry HL covers significantly more content than SL — additional depth in energetics (Born-Haber cycles, Gibbs free energy), kinetics (rate expressions and mechanisms), equilibrium (Ka, buffer solutions), and a substantially expanded organic chemistry section including advanced reaction mechanisms and spectroscopic identification. HL students also face a longer Paper 2. The content depth at HL is comparable to first-year university chemistry.
The IB Chemistry data booklet is provided in Papers 2 and 3 and contains standard electrode potentials, Ka values, the periodic table, thermodynamic data, spectroscopic data, and other reference values. Students do not need to memorise values that are in the data booklet, but they must know it thoroughly and be able to locate and use the relevant data quickly under exam conditions. We train students to use the data booklet efficiently as part of our exam preparation.
The option topic is usually chosen by the school. The most commonly taught options in Dubai IB schools are Medicinal Chemistry and Biochemistry. We support all four option topics and recommend treating the option as seriously as the core content — it represents a significant portion of Paper 3 marks.
The IA is worth 20% of the final Chemistry grade. A strong IA — scoring well on Exploration, Analysis, and Evaluation — can provide crucial marks that reduce the pressure on the written papers. Many students underperform on the IA because they do not understand the IB criteria or rush the write-up. We ensure every student we support produces an IA that genuinely maximises their marks.
Yes — we regularly take on students who are significantly behind, either because of gaps from MYP, weak school teaching, or a difficult start to the IB. We conduct an initial assessment of each student's current level, identify priority gaps, and build a structured catch-up programme alongside ongoing school support. Significant improvements are consistently achievable with focused, expert tutoring.

1. Home
  2. /Secondary Curriculum

  4. /Physics

IB Physics

IB Physics HL and SL — uncertainty and data analysis mastery, all four option topics, IA support, and past paper programmes for Engineering and Physical Sciences.

Book Free AssessmentView All Physics Courses
OVERVIEW

What is IB Physics?

IB Physics is one of the most challenging and most rewarding IB subjects, developing deep understanding of the physical world from classical mechanics through to quantum and nuclear physics. Higher Level Physics is particularly demanding — it extends significantly beyond SL with additional content in wave phenomena, electromagnetic induction, and quantum and nuclear physics, while also requiring greater mathematical rigour throughout. IB Physics is essential for students intending to study Engineering, Physics, Medicine, or Physical Sciences at university. In Dubai, where many students at IB World Schools aim for competitive university programmes, strong IB Physics grades are increasingly high-stakes. At Improve ME, our specialist IB Physics tutors cover both HL and SL with dedicated IA mentoring, option topic preparation, and full past paper programmes.

IB
Qualification
Ages 16-18
Age range
SL & HL
Exam boards
COURSE CONTENT

IB Physics Course Content

SL & HL coverage plus Internal Assessment (IA) support and exam strategy.

Core Topics (HL and SL)

  • •Measurements and Uncertainties — SI units, fundamental and derived units, scientific notation, random and systematic errors, uncertainty propagation, graphical analysis with error bars
  • •Mechanics — kinematics (SUVAT), Newton's laws, friction, work, energy, power, conservation of momentum, impulse, projectile motion, circular motion, gravitational fields — Newton's law of gravitation, gravitational potential, orbital motion
  • •Thermal Physics — thermal concepts, kinetic model of an ideal gas, ideal gas law, thermodynamic processes, specific heat capacity, latent heat
  • •Waves — wave properties, superposition, standing waves, Doppler effect, electromagnetic spectrum, reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference
  • •Electricity and Magnetism — electric fields and potential, Coulomb's law, capacitance, current electricity, Ohm's law, resistivity, magnetic fields, magnetic force on charges and conductors
  • •Atomic, Nuclear, and Particle Physics — atomic structure, radioactive decay, nuclear reactions, binding energy, mass-energy equivalence, fundamental particles and the standard model

HL Additional Topics

  • •Wave Phenomena (HL only) — single-slit diffraction, resolution (Rayleigh criterion), double-slit interference, thin-film interference, polarisation
  • •Fields (HL only) — combining gravitational and electric field concepts, gravitational potential energy, escape velocity, orbital energies, electric potential energy, capacitors in more depth
  • •Electromagnetic Induction (HL only) — Faraday's law, Lenz's law, alternating current generation, transformers, rectification, RLC circuits, resonance
  • •Quantum and Nuclear Physics (HL only) — photoelectric effect, matter waves, de Broglie wavelength, wave-particle duality, atomic spectra, Schrödinger model, Heisenberg uncertainty principle, nuclear models, radioactive decay law, nuclear stability

Internal Assessment, Options, and Data Analysis

  • •Physics IA — individual investigation worth 20%; assessed on Personal Engagement, Exploration, Analysis, Evaluation, and Communication; student-designed experiment
  • •IA topic selection — choosing an investigable, original topic with clear independent and dependent variables and sufficient scope for analysis and evaluation
  • •Option topics — choose one: Relativity (special and general relativity, spacetime), Engineering Physics (rigid body mechanics, fluids, thermodynamics), Imaging (optics, medical imaging), Astrophysics (stellar physics, cosmology, the universe)
  • •Uncertainty calculations — absolute, fractional, and percentage uncertainties; propagating uncertainties through calculations; error bars on graphs; evaluation of reliability
  • •Paper 3 data analysis — Section A: unfamiliar experimental data presented for analysis; requires processing raw data, drawing graphs, calculating gradients, and evaluating methodology
  • •Mathematical skills — solving simultaneous equations, use of logarithms in graphs (log-log and semi-log), trigonometry, vector resolution throughout all papers
ASSESSMENT

Assessment Structure

We support both Internal Assessment (IA) and final exams with a structured plan, feedback cycles, and timed practice. SL and HL requirements are clearly differentiated.

Paper 1
Multiple Choice — Conceptual Recall
  • •SL: 45 mins, 20% — 30 MCQ; tests quick conceptual recall and eliminates common misconceptions; no data booklet on Paper 1.
  • •HL: 60 mins, 20% — 40 MCQ; same focus with greater breadth.
Paper 2
Short & Extended Response
  • •SL: 75 mins, 40%; HL: 135 mins, 36% — short and extended response; includes calculations, mechanism drawing (Chemistry), and 8–10 mark extended response questions requiring full working and explanation.
  • •Data booklet provided for Papers 2 and 3; students must know how to use it efficiently.
Paper 3 + IA
Data Analysis & Option + IA (20%)
  • •SL: Paper 3 — 60 mins, 20% (data analysis + option topic); HL: 75 mins, 24%.
  • •Section A: unfamiliar experimental data for analysis; processing raw data, graphs, gradients, evaluation of methodology.
  • •Physics IA: individual investigation worth 20%; assessed on Personal Engagement, Exploration, Analysis, Evaluation, and Communication.
EXAM BOARDS

Assessment Framework

IB DP (SL)
IB DP (HL)
Internal Assessment (IA)

Not sure which board your child is on? We'll check at your free assessment.

TEACHING APPROACH

Our IB Physics Teaching Approach

01

HL vs SL Content Separation

HL Physics requires mastery of four additional topic areas that go significantly beyond SL. We teach HL and SL content separately and never conflate the two — ensuring HL students are fully prepared for the additional depth and mathematical rigour without overwhelming SL students.

02

Uncertainty and Data Analysis Mastery

Uncertainty calculations and data analysis appear in the IA, Paper 3, and throughout Paper 2. We teach these systematically — absolute, fractional, and percentage uncertainties, error propagation, graphical analysis, and experimental evaluation — until students can apply them confidently to any dataset.

03

Option Topic Deep Dive

The option topic in Paper 3 is a significant mark opportunity that many students underprepare for. We deliver dedicated sessions on whichever option the student's school has chosen — Relativity, Engineering Physics, Imaging, or Astrophysics — covering all content and past paper questions.

04

IA Design and Execution Support

A well-designed Physics IA can earn full marks in Exploration and Analysis. We help students choose appropriate topics, design valid experimental procedures, plan data collection, process results correctly, and write evaluations that earn maximum marks.

WHY CHOOSE US

Why Improve ME for IB Physics?

IB Physics specialists at both HL and SL
All four HL additional topics covered — Wave Phenomena, Fields, Electromagnetic Induction, Quantum and Nuclear
All four option topics supported — Relativity, Engineering Physics, Imaging, Astrophysics
IA topic selection, experimental design, data analysis, and write-up support
Paper 3 Section A data analysis and uncertainty preparation
Mathematical skills integration throughout all sessions
Strong outcomes for Engineering and Physical Sciences university applicants
Past paper programmes aligned to current IB Physics syllabus
one-to-one and small group sessions available
KHDA-approved tutoring centre at Gold & Diamond Park, Dubai
FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

Everything you need to know about Physics at Improve ME

HL Physics includes four additional topics — Wave Phenomena, Fields, Electromagnetic Induction, and Quantum and Nuclear Physics — that add significant content and mathematical complexity beyond SL. HL students also have a longer Paper 2 and a more demanding Paper 1. The grade 7 boundary for HL Physics is typically lower than SL in percentage terms, reflecting the greater difficulty, but the absolute level of knowledge required is substantially higher.
The option topic is usually chosen by the school rather than the individual student. The most commonly taught options in Dubai IB schools are Astrophysics and Relativity. We support all four options and recommend students study their option topic thoroughly rather than treating it as less important — it represents a significant portion of Paper 3 marks.
The IA is worth 20% of the final IB Physics grade — equivalent to one full paper. A well-executed IA that earns high marks in Exploration, Analysis, and Evaluation can provide a significant grade buffer for students who find the written papers challenging. Conversely, a poorly executed IA that scores low on criteria can be very difficult to recover from in the papers alone.
Yes — uncertainty calculations appear in the IA, throughout Paper 3 Section A, and in some Paper 2 questions. Students who cannot handle uncertainties correctly lose marks consistently across multiple components. We treat uncertainty mastery as a non-negotiable foundation skill and cover it systematically early in every student's programme.
Yes — we can deliver the full IB Physics programme from scratch for both HL and SL students. This includes all core and HL additional topics, the chosen option topic, full IA support, and complete past paper preparation for all three papers. This is one of the most common situations we support.

IB Economics

IB Economics HL and SL — diagram mastery, IA commentary coaching, chain of reasoning, Paper 3 quantitative preparation for PPE and Finance applicants.

Book Free AssessmentView All Economics Courses
OVERVIEW

What is IB Economics?

IB Economics combines microeconomic and macroeconomic theory with global real-world application, developing students' ability to analyse markets, evaluate government policies, and understand how international economics shapes the world. The course is assessed through a combination of written papers and an Internal Assessment portfolio of three economic commentaries. Higher Level Economics adds further depth in microeconomic theory — market structures, theory of the firm, and market failure — alongside additional macroeconomic content. IB Economics is highly regarded by universities for PPE, Economics, Finance, and International Relations degree programmes. At Improve ME, our IB Economics tutors build the diagram mastery, chain-of-reasoning skills, and IA commentary technique that distinguish 6 and 7 candidates from the rest of the field.

IB
Qualification
Ages 16-18
Age range
SL & HL
Exam boards
COURSE CONTENT

IB Economics Course Content

SL & HL coverage plus Internal Assessment (IA) support and exam strategy.

Microeconomics

  • •Competitive markets — demand and supply, market equilibrium, price mechanism, shifts in demand and supply, consumer and producer surplus, allocative efficiency
  • •Elasticity — price elasticity of demand (calculation, determinants, total revenue), price elasticity of supply, income elasticity, cross elasticity — applications and implications
  • •Government intervention — indirect taxes, subsidies, price ceilings and floors — impact on welfare, consumer and producer surplus, deadweight loss
  • •Market failure — public goods (non-excludable and non-rival), merit and demerit goods, positive and negative externalities, common pool resources, asymmetric information
  • •Theory of the firm (HL) — short run and long run production, costs (fixed, variable, average, marginal), economies and diseconomies of scale, profit maximisation (MR = MC)
  • •Market structures (HL) — perfect competition, monopolistic competition, oligopoly (kinked demand curve, game theory, collusion), monopoly — diagrams, efficiency, and evaluation
  • •Labour markets (HL) — demand for labour, wage determination, monopsony, minimum wage, trade unions, discrimination

Macroeconomics

  • •Measuring national income — GDP calculation methods (expenditure, income, output), GNI, purchasing power parity, limitations of GDP as a welfare measure, HDI and alternative measures
  • •Aggregate demand and supply — AD components and determinants, SRAS and LRAS, output gaps, economic cycle, demand and supply-side shocks
  • •Macroeconomic objectives — economic growth (actual and potential), low inflation (CPI measurement, demand-pull and cost-push causes), low unemployment (types and natural rate), balance of payments equilibrium
  • •Fiscal policy — government expenditure and taxation, budget positions, multiplier effect, automatic stabilisers, supply-side fiscal policy — advantages and limitations
  • •Monetary policy — interest rates and money supply, central bank independence, quantitative easing, inflation targeting — transmission mechanisms and limitations
  • •Supply-side policies — labour market reforms, privatisation, deregulation, education and training investment — evaluation of effectiveness
  • •International trade — comparative advantage, terms of trade, free trade arguments, protectionism (tariffs, quotas, subsidies) — costs and benefits, WTO, trading blocs

IA Commentaries and Global Economy

  • •Internal Assessment portfolio — three commentaries on different sections of the syllabus, each based on a different published news article; maximum 800 words per commentary
  • •Commentary structure — introduction with theoretical context, diagram, analysis of the article using economic theory, evaluation of multiple perspectives, conclusion
  • •Diagram selection and drawing — choosing the appropriate diagram for the article's content; drawing and annotating it correctly with specific labels and shifts
  • •Key concepts — scarcity, choice, efficiency, equity, economic well-being, sustainability, change, interdependence, intervention, markets — must be explicitly linked in at least one commentary
  • •Development economics — measuring development (GDP, GNI per capita, HDI, HPI), factors constraining development, strategies (trade vs aid, import substitution, export-led growth, IMF and World Bank)
  • •Globalisation — benefits and costs, impact on income distribution, multinational corporations, financial globalisation, global commons
ASSESSMENT

Assessment Structure

We support both Internal Assessment (IA) and final exams with a structured plan, feedback cycles, and timed practice. SL and HL requirements are clearly differentiated.

Paper 1
Extended Response — Micro & Macro
  • •SL: 75 mins, 30% — extended response; two questions each with two parts (part a and part b); both parts must be answered; evaluative essay with diagram expected.
  • •HL: 105 mins, 30% — same format; longer time for deeper analysis.
Paper 2
Data Response
  • •SL & HL: 105 mins; SL 40%, HL 30% — data response; two questions based on stimulus material; tests application of theory to real-world data.
Paper 3 (HL only) + IA
Policy Analysis + IA Portfolio
  • •HL only: Paper 3 — 105 mins, 20% — policy analysis questions requiring quantitative (elasticity, multiplier, index numbers) and qualitative analysis.
  • •IA portfolio: three commentaries (max 800 words each), each on a different syllabus section and different article; SL 30%, HL 20%.
EXAM BOARDS

Assessment Framework

IB DP (SL)
IB DP (HL)
Internal Assessment (IA)

Not sure which board your child is on? We'll check at your free assessment.

TEACHING APPROACH

Our IB Economics Teaching Approach

01

Diagram Mastery

Diagrams are worth marks in virtually every Economics question. We drill all key diagrams — supply and demand with welfare analysis, externalities with deadweight loss, AD/AS with output gaps, cost curves, market structure diagrams — until students can draw and annotate them perfectly under time pressure with correct labelling and shifts.

02

IA Commentary Coaching

The IA portfolio is worth 20–30% of the final grade and consists of three commentaries that must each use different sections of the syllabus. We coach students through article selection, diagram choice, commentary structure, economic analysis, and evaluation — with detailed written feedback on each draft against the IB criteria.

03

Chain of Reasoning Development

IB Economics mark schemes reward extended chains of economic reasoning — moving from cause through mechanism to effect with theory, diagrams, and real-world context. We develop this skill explicitly in every session rather than relying on students to acquire it naturally.

04

Paper 3 HL Quantitative Preparation

Paper 3 HL includes quantitative questions — elasticity calculations, multiplier calculations, index numbers — that many HL students underprepare for. We ensure all HL students are confident with the required mathematical skills well before the exam.

WHY CHOOSE US

Why Improve ME for IB Economics?

IB Economics specialists at both HL and SL
HL additional content — Theory of the Firm, market structures, labour markets, Paper 3
IA commentary coaching — article selection, structure, diagrams, and evaluation
Diagram mastery training across all key micro and macro diagrams
Chain of reasoning development — the core analytical skill
Paper 3 HL quantitative skills preparation
Development economics and globalisation in depth
Strong PPE, Economics, and Finance applicant outcomes
one-to-one and small group sessions available
KHDA-approved tutoring centre at Gold & Diamond Park, Dubai
FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

Everything you need to know about Economics at Improve ME

The IA portfolio consists of three commentaries, each based on a different published news or media article, and each addressing a different section of the syllabus — one micro, one macro, and one from international or development economics. Each commentary is a maximum of 800 words plus a diagram. The portfolio is worth 20% of the HL grade and 30% of the SL grade.
Paper 3 is an HL-only paper worth 20% of the final grade. It includes a mixture of short quantitative questions and longer written responses based on an extract. The quantitative component tests calculations including elasticity, multiplier, index numbers, and terms of trade — areas that many HL students underprepare for. We treat Paper 3 preparation as a specific and important part of our HL Economics programme.
Diagrams are essential — they appear in Paper 1 essays (where a diagram is expected in part b responses), Paper 2 data response questions, and IA commentaries. A well-drawn, correctly labelled diagram with accurate shifts and welfare areas can earn marks directly and supports the written analysis around it. A missing or incorrect diagram in a question that expects one will always cost marks.
Yes — article selection is one of the most important IA decisions students make. The article must be current, from a reputable source, clearly connected to a specific section of the syllabus, and have sufficient economic content to support 800 words of analysis. We help students identify appropriate articles, evaluate whether they are suitable, and ensure their three commentaries cover different syllabus sections as required.
HL Economics adds substantial additional content in microeconomics — Theory of the Firm, detailed market structures including oligopoly and game theory, and labour market theory — as well as Paper 3 with its quantitative component. HL students also have a longer Paper 1. However, the IA is worth less (20% vs 30%), which means the written papers carry more weight. Most strong Economics students find HL manageable with proper support.

1. Home

IB Psychology

IB Psychology HL and SL — study knowledge, ERQ essay framework, IA experimental design, Paper 3 qualitative methods, and command term mastery.

Book Free AssessmentView All Psychology Courses
OVERVIEW

What is IB Psychology?

IB Psychology examines human behaviour and mental processes through three core approaches — biological, cognitive, and sociocultural — developing students' ability to describe, analyse, and evaluate psychological research with scientific rigour. Higher Level Psychology adds qualitative research methods and an additional HL extension topic, requiring students to engage with a broader range of research methodologies. The course demands precise study knowledge, sophisticated evaluation, and the ability to construct well-structured essays and short answer responses under time pressure. IB Psychology is increasingly popular among Dubai IB students and provides excellent preparation for university Psychology, Medicine, Social Sciences, and Education degrees. At Improve ME, our IB Psychology tutors build the study depth, evaluative sophistication, and essay technique that IB examiners consistently reward with 6s and 7s.

IB
Qualification
Ages 16-18
Age range
SL & HL
Exam boards
COURSE CONTENT

IB Psychology Course Content

SL & HL coverage plus Internal Assessment (IA) support and exam strategy.

The Three Core Approaches

  • •Biological Approach — neurotransmitters (serotonin, dopamine, acetylcholine) and their role in behaviour, hormones and pheromones, genetics and behaviour (twin studies, adoption studies, MAOA gene), evolution and behaviour (natural selection, mate choice), the brain and behaviour (localisation, neuroplasticity, techniques for studying the brain — fMRI, EEG, lesion studies)
  • •Cognitive Approach — cognitive processing (schema theory, dual processing), models of memory (multi-store model, working memory model), thinking and decision making (System 1 and 2, heuristics and biases, Kahneman), emotion and cognition (flashbulb memories, mood-congruent memory), cognitive development (Piaget's stages, Vygotsky's ZPD, theory of mind)
  • •Sociocultural Approach — the individual and the group (social identity theory, social cognitive theory, stereotypes, prejudice, discrimination), cultural origins of behaviour (Hofstede's dimensions, cultural norms, acculturation, Berry's acculturation framework), cultural influences on individual behaviour (bystander effect, conformity, obedience, social learning)

Applied Topics and HL Extensions

  • •Abnormal Psychology — concepts of normality and abnormality, classification systems (DSM-5 and ICD-11), validity and reliability of diagnosis, etiology (biological, cognitive, sociocultural explanations), treatment approaches (biological — drug therapy; psychological — CBT, mindfulness; sociocultural — group therapy), one disorder in depth (depression, OCD, PTSD, or eating disorders depending on teacher choice)
  • •Developmental Psychology — cognitive development (Piaget, Vygotsky, Bruner), social development (Bowlby's attachment, Strange Situation, cultural variations), identity development (Erikson's stages, adolescent identity), moral development (Kohlberg, Gilligan)
  • •Health Psychology — health and well-being models, stress (biological and psychological), stress management, substance use and misuse (explanations and treatment), health promotion
  • •Qualitative Research Methods (HL) — case studies, interviews (structured, semi-structured, unstructured), observations, thematic analysis, credibility and reflexivity, ethical considerations in qualitative research
  • •HL Extension — one additional topic studied in depth with additional assessment: psychology of human relationships, conflict and peacemaking, psychology and health, or cognitive processing in the digital world

Research Methods and Assessment Skills

  • •Experimental methods — laboratory, field, natural, and quasi-experiments; independent, dependent, and confounding variables; experimental and control groups; random allocation
  • •Non-experimental methods — correlational studies, observations (naturalistic and controlled), case studies, interviews, surveys — strengths and limitations of each
  • •Ethical considerations — informed consent, deception, debriefing, right to withdraw, protection from harm, confidentiality — applying ethics to specific studies
  • •Essay technique — Short Answer Responses (SAR): 200–250 words describing a study or concept; Extended Response Questions (ERQ): 800–1000 words evaluating a topic across multiple studies
  • •Evaluating research — discussing methodology, ethical issues, cultural bias, gender bias, reductionism, determinism, application to real-world behaviour
  • •Command terms mastery — distinguish between "describe", "explain", "discuss", "evaluate", "contrast", and "to what extent" — each requires a different response structure
ASSESSMENT

Assessment Structure

We support both Internal Assessment (IA) and final exams with a structured plan, feedback cycles, and timed practice. SL and HL requirements are clearly differentiated.

Paper 1
Core Approaches + ERQ
  • •SL: 120 mins, 50% — Short Answer Responses (SAR) on all three approaches (Biological, Cognitive, Sociocultural) + one Extended Response Question (ERQ).
  • •HL: 120 mins, 40% — SAR on all three approaches + two ERQs; ERQs are 800–1000 words, evaluative essays across multiple studies.
Paper 2
Applied Topics
  • •SL: 60 mins, 25% — one ERQ from three applied topics (Abnormal, Developmental, Health Psychology).
  • •HL: 60 mins, 20% — same format.
Paper 3 (HL only) + IA
Qualitative Methods + IA (20–25%)
  • •HL only: Paper 3 — 60 mins, 20% — unseen qualitative study; methodological and ethical questions; rewards students specifically taught qualitative research methods.
  • •IA: experimental study (replicate or extend published study); ~2,000 words; 20% HL, 25% SL; assessed on introduction, exploration, analysis, evaluation, presentation.
EXAM BOARDS

Assessment Framework

IB DP (SL)
IB DP (HL)
Internal Assessment (IA)

Not sure which board your child is on? We'll check at your free assessment.

TEACHING APPROACH

Our IB Psychology Teaching Approach

01

Study Knowledge and Precise Description

IB Psychology requires students to know specific studies in detail — not just Milgram and Asch, but a range of biological, cognitive, and sociocultural studies across all three approaches. We build comprehensive study knowledge systematically and train students to describe study methodology, results, and conclusions precisely within the SAR word limit.

02

ERQ Essay Mastery

Extended Response Questions are the highest-mark items on all Psychology papers and require structured evaluation across multiple studies and perspectives. We teach a specific ERQ framework — introduction with focus, body paragraphs each addressing the question from a different angle with study evidence, conclusion with overall evaluation — and practise under timed conditions.

03

HL Paper 3 Preparation

Paper 3 presents an unseen qualitative study and asks students to evaluate its methodology and ethical considerations. We train HL students specifically in qualitative research methods and practise Paper 3-style responses until they can confidently analyse any qualitative study presented to them.

04

IA Experimental Design

The Psychology IA requires students to replicate or extend a published study, collect data, and write up the results. We help students select an appropriate study to replicate, design a valid methodology, conduct basic statistical analysis, and write a clear, structured report that meets all IB assessment criteria.

WHY CHOOSE US

Why Improve ME for IB Psychology?

IB Psychology specialists at both HL and SL
All three core approaches covered in depth — Biological, Cognitive, Sociocultural
All applied topics supported — Abnormal, Developmental, Health Psychology
HL qualitative methods and Paper 3 preparation
ERQ essay framework coaching — the highest-mark skill in IB Psychology
SAR precision training — correct study knowledge within word limits
IA experimental design, data collection, and write-up support
Command term mastery — knowing exactly how to answer every question type
one-to-one and small group sessions available
KHDA-approved tutoring centre at Gold & Diamond Park, Dubai
FAQ

Frequently Asked Questions

Everything you need to know about Psychology at Improve ME

There is no fixed number, but students need to know a range of studies across all three core approaches and their chosen applied topic — typically 3–5 studies per topic area. Importantly, students need to know each study in depth — the aim of the study, the procedure, the results, the conclusion, and evaluative points including methodological strengths and limitations, ethical issues, and cultural or gender bias. Surface-level study knowledge is one of the most common reasons students underperform.
A Short Answer Response (SAR) is a 200–250 word response that typically asks students to describe or explain a concept, study, or finding. An Extended Response Question (ERQ) is an 800–1000 word evaluative essay that asks students to discuss or evaluate a broader topic using multiple studies and perspectives. ERQs are the highest-mark items on the papers and require a different structure and approach to SARs.
Paper 3 is an HL-only paper in which students are presented with an unseen qualitative study and must answer questions about its research methodology and ethical considerations. Questions typically ask students to identify the research method used, discuss its strengths and limitations, consider ethical issues, and evaluate the credibility of the study. This paper rewards students who have been specifically taught qualitative research methods.
The IB Psychology IA is an experimental study in which students replicate or extend a published psychological study. Students design the methodology, collect data from a small sample, conduct a basic statistical analysis (typically a Mann-Whitney U test or a chi-squared test), and write a report of approximately 2,000 words. The IA is worth 20–25% of the final grade and is marked against specific IB criteria covering introduction, exploration, analysis, evaluation, and presentation.
Yes — IB Psychology is widely accepted as a relevant qualification for Psychology, Medicine, Social Sciences, Education, and Nursing degree applications. For competitive Psychology programmes, particularly those with scientific admissions requirements, the biological and research methods content of IB Psychology is valued. However, individual university entry requirements vary and we always advise students to check specific requirements for their target courses.

IB DIPLOMA PROGRAMME

IB Business Studies

Ages 16–19 (Years 12–13)

IB Business Studies develops students' ability to apply business management theories and tools to real-world contexts. Whether studying at Standard Level or Higher Level, our tutor helps students build the analytical and evaluative writing skills that examiners reward — from structured responses to case study analysis.

The subject is assessed through a combination of written examinations and an Internal Assessment (IA). At Higher Level, students also complete an additional paper and extended case study analysis. At Standard Level, the focus is on core concepts and structured responses across two papers.

Book Free Assessment →View IB Curriculum →

Trusted by families across Dubai since 2010 · KHDA-approved

PARENT GUIDE

What is IB Business Studies? A Parent's Guide

Understanding the subject, the assessment, and what strong results actually require

IB Business Studies develops students' ability to apply business management theories and tools to real-world contexts. Whether studying at Standard Level or Higher Level, our tutor helps students build the analytical and evaluative writing skills that examiners reward — from structured responses to case study analysis.

The subject is assessed through a combination of written examinations and an Internal Assessment (IA). At Higher Level, students also complete an additional paper and extended case study analysis. At Standard Level, the focus is on core concepts and structured responses across two papers.

What makes IB Business Studies genuinely challenging is the level of analytical depth required. It is not enough to describe a concept — examiners expect students to evaluate, apply real business tools, and construct well-reasoned arguments. Our tutors teach the difference between a response that describes and a response that earns a 7.

Key Facts

IB Year 1 & Year 2 (Years 12–13)

Ages 16–19

Higher Level and Standard Level

HL & SL

IA + 2–3 Exam Papers

Key Assessment

AT A GLANCE

IB Business Studies at a Glance

IB Year 1 & Year 2 (Years 12–13)

Studied across two years as part of the IB Diploma Programme, assessed through final exams and an Internal Assessment.

Higher Level and Standard Level

HL includes an additional paper and greater analytical depth. SL focuses on core units across Papers 1 and 2.

IA + 2–3 Exam Papers

The Internal Assessment is a research project worth 25% of the final grade. Written papers assess case study analysis and structured responses.

WHY IT MATTERS

Why IB Business Studies Results Matter

IB Business Studies is a popular subject at leading Dubai IB schools including GEMS World Academy, Jumeirah College, and Dubai International Academy. Cohorts are large and competition is strong — meaning the difference between a 5 and a 7 often comes down to exam technique and IA quality, not knowledge alone.

Students at Dubai schools following the IB Diploma Programme need structured support to move from surface-level answers to the higher-order analytical responses needed for Grades 5, 6, and 7. Strong results open direct pathways to Business, Economics, Finance, and Management programmes at top universities.

The IA makes or breaks borderline grades

The Internal Assessment contributes 25% of the final grade. A well-executed IA can lift a student from a 5 to a 6 — or from a 6 to a 7. We guide students from research question selection through to the final submission.

Command terms determine exam marks

IB examiners reward precision. 'Examine', 'evaluate', and 'justify' each require a different type of response. We train students to identify command terms instantly and structure their answers accordingly.

SUBJECTS

What We Cover in IB Business Studies

Full syllabus coverage across all five units, at both HL and SL

Unit 1: Business Organisation & Environment

Types of organisations, stakeholders, business objectives, growth and evolution, and the external environment — applied to real business contexts.

HL & SL
Learn More →

Unit 2: Human Resource Management

Organisational structure, leadership styles, motivation theory, and organisational culture — built into structured exam responses.

HL & SL
Learn More →

Unit 3: Finance & Accounts

Financial statements, ratio analysis, investment appraisal, and sources of finance. One of the most technically demanding units.

HL & SL
Learn More →

Unit 4: Marketing

Market research, segmentation, the marketing mix, and digital marketing — with case study application built into every topic.

HL & SL
Learn More →

Unit 5: Operations Management

Production methods, quality management, supply chain, and lean production. Often underestimated — we make sure it is not.

HL & SL
Learn More →

HL Extension: Investment Appraisal, Organisational Change & Crisis Management

HL students cover investment appraisal, organisational change, and crisis management — plus a pre-seen case study for Paper 3. We prepare students thoroughly for the extended analysis this requires.

HL only
Learn More →

METHODOLOGY

How We Prepare Students for IB Business Studies

Structured, examiner-focused preparation from Year 1 through to final papers

IB Business Studies rewards students who can think analytically, apply business tools accurately, and write with precision under exam conditions. Our preparation is built around exactly those skills.

01

Small Groups (Max 6 Students)

All sessions run in small groups of no more than 6 students, with separate sessions structured around HL and SL syllabus content.

This keeps teaching focused and allows the tutor to tailor explanations to the level each student is sitting — without HL-only content distracting SL students, or vice versa.

02

Command Term Focus

Heavy focus on the command terms IB examiners use — Explain, Analyse, Evaluate, Discuss — and how each demands a different type of response.

Most students know the content. The ones who score 7s know how to deploy it. We close that gap.

03

Past Paper Practice with Structured Feedback

We work through past papers using real IB mark schemes so students understand exactly what earns marks and why.

Timed practice, structured feedback, and mark scheme comparison are built into every stage of preparation — from early revision through to final exam readiness.

04

Case Study Technique

We teach students to apply business tools — SWOT, Ansoff Matrix, and others — in exam answers rather than just describing them.

IB examiners reward application and evaluation, not description. We train students to read an unseen case study, identify the key issue, select the right framework, and construct an evaluative response under time pressure.

EXAM BOARDS

Assessment Structure We Cover

Full alignment with the IB Diploma Programme Business Studies guide

Internal Assessment (HL & SL) — Individual Research ProjectPaper 1 — Pre-seen Case Study (HL & SL)Paper 2 — Unseen Data Response (HL & SL)Paper 3 — Unseen Case Study with Extended Response (HL only)May & November Sessions

Not sure which board your child is on? We'll check at your free assessment.

HOW WE TEACH

How we teach IB Business Studies Tutoring in Dubai | Improve ME Institute

01

HL vs SL Distinction

HL students receive targeted preparation for Paper 3 and the additional HL content. SL students build depth across Papers 1 and 2 without the distraction of HL-only material.

02

Case Study Technique

Business Studies is fundamentally a case study subject. We teach students to apply business tools (SWOT, Ansoff, etc.) in exam answers — not just describe them — and construct evaluative conclusions.

03

IA Support

We guide students through the individual research project from research question to final submission, ensuring methodology, business tool application, and analysis all meet IB criteria.

04

Economics Overlap

The same tutor covers Economics — useful for students taking both subjects, as concepts overlap across market structures, demand and supply, and financial analysis.

WHY IMPROVE ME

Why Improve ME for IB Business Studies?

Small groups of max 6 students — separate HL and SL sessions

Heavy focus on command terms: Explain, Analyse, Evaluate, Discuss

Past paper practice with structured feedback and real IB mark schemes

Case study technique — applying business tools in exam answers, not just describing them

Full Internal Assessment support from research question to final submission

Paper 3 preparation for HL students — unseen case study with extended response

Same tutor covers Economics — beneficial for students taking both subjects

KHDA-approved tutoring centre at Gold & Diamond Park, Dubai

SUCCESS STORIES

Business Courses

Explore

FAQ

IB Business Studies — Frequently Asked Questions

Both HL and SL cover the same five core units. HL students additionally cover investment appraisal, organisational change, and crisis management, and sit Paper 3 — an unseen case study with an extended response. HL requires a higher level of analytical depth. At Improve ME, we run separate sessions for HL and SL so each level receives the focused preparation it needs.
The IA is an individual research project on a real business. It contributes significantly to the final grade, making it one of the most important components of the course. We support students through every stage — from selecting a workable research question through to completing the final submission — ensuring the methodology is sound and business tools are applied correctly.
This is one of the most common issues we see in IB Business Studies. The gap between a 5 and a 7 is almost always about exam technique, not knowledge. IB examiners reward specific skills — applying command terms correctly, using business frameworks in context (not just describing them), and constructing evaluative arguments. We train students on exactly these skills until they become second nature.
Ideally at the start of IB Year 1 so that IA skills and exam technique are built up progressively alongside the syllabus. That said, students who join in Year 2 still make significant progress — particularly with focused past paper and mark scheme work in the months before exams.
Yes — the same tutor covers both subjects, which is beneficial for students taking both. There is meaningful conceptual overlap between Business Studies and Economics across market structures, demand and supply, and financial analysis. Students find that reinforcing both subjects together improves their performance in each.

Why Is Career Guidance So Important for Students?

Curriculum

British Curriculum Syllabus Explained: What Your Child Will Study in Dubai Schools

2024-12-064 min read

If you're living in Dubai and looking for the best school curriculum for your child, you've probably heard about the British Curriculum. But one simple question comes to every parent's mind: "What is the British Curriculum syllabus really like? What exactly will my child study?" Let's break it down in a simple way—no complicated words, just straight answers.

What Is the British Curriculum?

The British Curriculum is the official education system used in the UK. Many schools in Dubai follow it—like GEMS, Horizon English School, Dubai British School, and more.

It's a structured system divided into Key Stages, which means each age group has a clear and balanced syllabus to follow.

If your child ever feels lost or behind in this system, enrolling them in the right tutoring centre can make a huge difference.

Key Stages & Syllabus – What Your Child Will Study

1. EYFS (Ages 3–5) – Nursery and FS2

This is the beginning stage, focused on learning through play. Children learn through fun activities like:

  • Basic letters and sounds (phonics)
  • Counting and number games
  • Colours and shapes
  • Social skills and emotions

It builds a strong foundation.

2. Key Stage 1 (Ages 5–7) – Year 1 and 2

Here, children start learning formal subjects such as:

  • English (reading, writing, grammar)
  • Maths (addition, subtraction)
  • Science (plants, animals, seasons)

This stage builds curiosity.

3. Key Stage 2 (Ages 7–11) – Year 3 to 6

Now the syllabus gets a bit more detailed:

  • History and Geography
  • Science experiments
  • Writing longer answers
  • Learning multiplication, division, and fractions

Children start learning more independently.

4. Key Stage 3 (Ages 11–14) – Year 7 to 9

This is where students explore more subjects:

  • Physics, Chemistry, and Biology
  • English Language and Literature
  • Computer Science
  • Optional subjects like Music or Drama

It prepares them for the next big step – GCSEs.

5. Key Stage 4 (Ages 14–16) – Year 10 and 11

Now students enter the GCSE stage. They study core subjects like:

  • English, Maths, and Science

And also choose optional subjects like Business, Psychology, ICT, or Art. They sit proper exams, and this stage shows how deep the British Curriculum really is.

At this point, many students start thinking seriously about their future careers.

6. Key Stage 5 (Ages 16–18) – A Levels

This is the final stage before university. Students choose 3–4 subjects they want to focus on, such as: Physics, Economics, Media, etc. This stage helps them prepare for college or future careers.

Why Is the British Curriculum Popular in Dubai?

  • The syllabus is clear and age-wise structured
  • Schools in Dubai follow KHDA rules
  • The curriculum is accepted in countries like the UK, Canada, and Australia
  • Dubai schools also offer great extra activities and international exposure

Why Should Parents Understand the Syllabus?

When you know what your child is studying:

  • You can help better at home
  • Choosing the right school becomes easier
  • Your child feels more confident and prepared

Need Help with the British Curriculum? We're Here for You

If you want your child to succeed in the British Curriculum – whether it's in the early years or at A-Levels – we've got your back.

Improve ME Institute offers:

  • Expert help for British Curriculum students
  • Extra support for any weak areas
  • GCSE and A-Level preparation
  • Homework guidance and study tips

Book a FREE consultation with Improve ME Institute today.

Frequently Asked Questions

Is the syllabus the same as in the UK?

Yes, it follows the UK system. Some local topics like Arabic or UAE Social Studies may be added in Dubai schools.

When do students start GCSEs?

Usually in Year 10 (age 14), completed by Year 11.

What's the difference between IB and British Curriculum?

British Curriculum goes deep into subjects (especially A-Levels), while IB covers more topics in less depth. Depends on your child's learning style.

Can my child switch to the British Curriculum from another system?

Yes, but there may be a short adjustment period. Improve ME Institute can support that transition smoothly.

Final Thoughts

The British Curriculum is structured, deep, and globally recognised. Dubai schools follow it very well, offering your child a great learning experience. And if you ever feel your child needs extra help—at any stage—Improve ME Institute is always ready to guide and support.

Study Tips

Top Tips to Manage Exam Stress

2025-01-064 min read

Even for the most prepared student, exams can be a stressful time. Whether GCSEs, A Levels, or even just an end-of-term paper, they are an essential part of education. Stress and nerves are the bread and butter of a student's routine, while aiming to stay focused and motivated; however, too much of either can impact their performance.

So how can students manage exam stress? We have taken a closer look at some top tips:

Create a plan

The saying "fail to prepare, prepare to fail" is very accurate when it comes to revision. That is why one of the best ways students can reduce stress around exam time is to ensure they are preparing in advance.

To do this, they should create a table of what exams they have, the dates they are on, and the topics they need to revise. This will ensure they can allocate enough time to cover everything required, leaving them feeling calmer and less stressed.

1. Avoid distractions

It can be very easy for students to become distracted when revising. Checking on social media or having the television on in the background is incredibly disruptive and reduces the quality of learning. That is why when creating their revision plan, students should allocate set blocks of thirty minutes where they avoid doing anything other than their studies. Once they have done that, they can pause for ten minutes before restarting.

2. Eat well and get active

As the exam draws closer, students must eat well and remain active. Exercise is a proven way to reduce stress, while the proper diet can help to improve brain power and focus.

3. Study together

Trying to study and revise alone can be lonely and only increase feelings of stress and anxiety. That is why students should look to create small study groups, allowing them to quiz each other on specific topics and support each other as needed.

4. Get enough sleep

With the nerves and stress around exam time, it can be hard to get enough rest. Students need to ensure that they get enough sleep ahead of the big day, as this will help them remain calm and focused on the tasks at hand.

5. Avoid the post-mortem

Stress does not end once the exam is over. Many students go over every question and answer in their heads, increasing the pressure. That is why they should go and take their mind off the exam by doing something fun that they will enjoy.

Get exam ready with Improve ME Institute

If your loved ones are stressed by exam time, then Improve ME Institute is here to help them. Tutoring is a great way to help give students the confidence to enter their exams free from stress.

We focus on providing in-depth lessons to primary and secondary-level students using our proven materials. We cover every subject on the curriculum, so get in touch with our team today to find out more.

Frequently Asked Questions

Why is exam stress so common among students?

Exams are an important part of education, and the pressure to perform well can naturally lead to stress and anxiety for many students.

How does creating a revision plan help reduce stress?

A revision plan helps students stay organised and manage their time effectively, reducing last-minute pressure and helping them feel more in control.

What are some effective ways to stay focused while revising?

Avoiding distractions such as social media and television, and studying in short, focused sessions with regular breaks, can greatly improve concentration.

Why are sleep and a healthy lifestyle important during exam time?

Getting enough sleep, eating well, and staying active help reduce stress, improve focus, and support overall exam performance.

How can tutoring help students feel more confident about exams?

Tutoring provides structured support and personalised guidance, helping students strengthen their understanding and approach exams with greater confidence.

Screen Time & Study Time: How to Find the Right Balance for Your Child

2025-01-206 min read

In today's digital world, screens are everywhere, used for learning, socialising, and entertainment. But as a parent, you've likely asked yourself: How much screen time is too much for my child? Finding the right balance between educational screen time and recreational use can feel overwhelming. The good news? Research-based strategies can help you set healthy limits while encouraging productive screen use that supports academic success.

The Impact of Screen Time on Learning and Focus

Studies show that excessive screen time can affect cognitive development and academic performance. A 2019 study published in JAMA Pediatrics found that children with more than two hours of daily recreational screen time had lower language and thinking scores.

Similarly, research from the National Institutes of Health (NIH) links extended screen exposure to reduced attention span and lower reading comprehension skills.

However, not all screen time is bad. A Harvard University study highlights that interactive learning apps and educational videos can enhance engagement and boost retention, especially when combined with hands-on learning. The key is to shift from passive screen consumption (like endless scrolling) to active learning experiences.

For students struggling with focus and academic planning, career counselling in Dubai can help them set structured study routines, improve time management, and develop better learning habits.

How to Balance Screen Time and Homework

1. Set Healthy Screen Time Limits

The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) recommends:

  • Ages 2-5: No more than one hour per day of high-quality programming.
  • Ages 6+: Set consistent limits based on schoolwork, physical activity, and family time.

Use parental control apps like Apple's Screen Time, Google's Family Link, or Qustodio to track and manage screen usage.

2. Encourage Screen Time for Learning

Not all screen time is created equal. Instead of endless TikTok scrolling, encourage your child to use educational platforms like:

  • Khan Academy (maths, science, and reading tutorials)
  • Duolingo (language learning)
  • Prodigy (gamified maths practice)
  • BrainPOP (animated educational videos)

These tools make studying more engaging while reinforcing academic concepts. If your child is uncertain about their future academic or career choices, career guidance for students can help them explore the right educational paths.

3. Create a Screen-Free Study Zone

A study from Common Sense Media found that children who keep their phones near them while studying are distracted up to 60% of the time. To improve focus, set up a dedicated homework space free from social media, gaming, and TV. If they need a device for research, consider blocking distracting websites using tools like Cold Turkey or Freedom.

4. Use Screens as a Reward, Not a Default

Instead of allowing unlimited recreational screen time, try using it as a motivational tool. For example, your child can earn 30 minutes of gaming or Netflix after completing their homework or chores. This reinforces time management skills while keeping screen time in check.

5. Lead by Example

Children model their behaviour after their parents. If they see you constantly checking emails or scrolling on your phone, they're likely to do the same. Establish tech-free family time during meals or before bedtime to encourage healthy digital habits.

How Improve ME Helps Students Balance Screens & Studies

At Improve ME Institute, we understand the challenge of balancing educational screen time with real-world learning. Our after-school tutoring programmes are designed to:

  • Provide structured, screen-conscious learning
  • Use interactive tools without distractions
  • Reinforce study habits that improve focus and time management

For students requiring additional academic guidance, our online student career counselling services can help them make informed decisions about their studies and future careers. By blending technology with hands-on tutoring, we help students stay engaged without falling into the trap of excessive screen use.

Book a Free Assessment Today

Want to help your child develop better study habits while managing screen time? Improve ME's after-school programmes can provide the structure and support they need. Sign up for a free assessment today!

Frequently Asked Questions

How much screen time is okay for my child?

The American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) recommends no more than one hour of quality screen time per day for kids aged 2-5. For older children, screen time should be balanced with schoolwork, physical activity, and quality family time. If your child struggles with time management, career guidance for students can help them develop better study habits.

Can screen time help my child learn?

Yes! Educational apps and videos, like Khan Academy and Duolingo, can make learning fun and interactive. At Improve ME Institute, we promote screen use that helps with school success while avoiding distractions.

How can I help my child stay focused while studying?

Set up a study space without social media, games, or TV. You can also use website blockers to prevent distractions. If your child needs help staying on track, student career counselling online can guide them in setting study goals.

Strategies For Tackling Homework and Submitting It on Time

2026-02-063 min read

Getting your children to complete their homework is a challenge that every parent will have faced at some point. With so many distractions at home, it can be hard to get your loved ones to focus and deliver their homework on time.

Having the right strategies in place will help to ensure that homework time is much smoother and that your child is able to deliver their work on time. What are the best strategies for tackling homework, though? We thought we would take a closer look…

Be organised

The first thing that you need to focus on is ensuring that you are organised. Make sure that you and your loved one know exactly what work needs to be completed, how it needs to be submitted and when it needs to be submitted by. Once you have this information, you will then be able to create a clear plan that you need to work towards.

Have a dedicated homework space

Once you know exactly what is required each week, the next step is to set up a dedicated homework space. This should be a quiet space away from distractions such as the television, toys and siblings. This will help to keep them focused on the task at hand and ensure that they can complete their work on time.

Break large projects down

When it comes to completing big homework projects, it can be a daunting prospect for both your child and sometimes parents as well. That is why one of the best things that you can do is to break the project down into smaller, more manageable tasks. This will make it easier for your child to focus and will ensure that the work is completed on time to the highest standards.

Establish breaks

No matter their age, every child needs to take an occasional break from studying and homework. Having them pause their homework every thirty minutes for a walk around, snack or short playtime will help keep them focused and motivated to complete the work.

Get help where needed

If your child is struggling with their homework and you are not sure how to help them, then do not be afraid to seek out the help of a professional tutor. Tutoring is a great way to ensure that your child can get the support and help that they need from someone who not only has a comprehensive understanding of the topic but also the wider curriculum.

1. Home
